items.title: Geographic Distribution of Coccidioidomycosis
items.content: Coccidioidomycosis, or Valley fever, is caused by the fungi genus Coccidioides and has a distinct geographic distribution. It is endemic to specific regions in the Western Hemisphere between latitudes 40°N and 40°S.
keyPoints: Endemic to areas between latitudes 40°N and 40°S.
In the U.S., high-risk areas include southern San Joaquin Valley, California, and south-central Arizona.
Cases have been reported outside traditional endemic regions due to changing climate.
items.reference: Coccidioidomycosis · Coccidioidomycosis | DEFINITION AND ETIOLOGY | EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.title: Epidemiological Trends in Coccidioidomycosis
items.content: The incidence of coccidioidomycosis is increasing, with notable cases reported from Arizona and California. Climatic conditions and human activities contribute to this trend.
keyPoints: Reported cases have been steadily increasing over the past two decades.
In 2019, there were 20,003 reported cases, primarily in Arizona and California.
Factors contributing to increased incidence include climate change, construction activity, and an aging population moving into endemic areas.
items.reference: Coccidioidomycosis · Coccidioidomycosis | DEFINITION AND ETIOLOGY | EPIDEMIOLOGY

items.title: Mechanism of Coccidioides Infection and Immune Response
items.content: Coccidioides organisms exist as filamentous molds in the environment. When arthroconidia are inhaled, they evade mucosal defenses and reach deep into the bronchial tree to initiate infection by enlarging into spherules. A robust cellular immune response is crucial for controlling coccidioidomycosis, with necrotizing granulomas containing spherules indicating resolved pulmonary infections.
keyPoints: Arthroconidia measure approximately 2 μm by 5 μm and become airborne.
Spherules can attain sizes up to 200 μm.
Necrotizing granulomas with spherules indicate controlled infection.
Poorly formed or absent granulomas are seen in uncontrolled, disseminated disease.
items.reference: Coccidioidomycosis · PATHOGENESIS, PATHOLOGY, AND IMMUNE RESPONSE

items.title: Role of Immune Response in Coccidioidomycosis Outcome
items.content: A robust cellular immune response is critical for controlling coccidioidomycosis. Patients with resolved pulmonary infections show necrotizing granulomas containing spherules. In disseminated disease, poorly formed or absent granulomas and a polymorphonuclear leukocyte response are common. Protective immunity involves prolonged presence of spherules in the lungs, but waning immunity can lead to recurrent active disease.
keyPoints: Robust cellular immune response is essential for controlling infection.
Necrotizing granulomas with spherules indicate resolved pulmonary infections.
Disseminated disease shows poorly formed or absent granulomas and polymorphonuclear leukocyte response.
Protective immunity involves prolonged presence of spherules, risking recurrent active disease if immunity wanes.
items.reference: Coccidioidomycosis · PATHOGENESIS, PATHOLOGY, AND IMMUNE RESPONSE

items.title: Recognizing Primary Pulmonary Coccidioidomycosis
items.content: Primary pulmonary coccidioidomycosis often mimics community-acquired bacterial pneumonia but can be distinguished by specific clinical features and exposure history.
keyPoints: 60% of infected individuals are asymptomatic; 40% develop symptoms like fever, cough, pleuritic chest pain within days to 3 weeks post-inhalation.
Symptoms increase with age.
Immunologic responses include rash (erythema nodosum or multiforme), arthralgias ('desert rheumatism'), and peripheral-blood eosinophilia.

Diagnosis is suggested by rash, symmetrical arthralgias, night sweats, marked fatigue, failure to improve with antibacterial therapy, and specific chest imaging findings (upper lobe infiltrate or hilar/mediastinal lymphadenopathy).
items.reference: Coccidioidomycosis · CLINICAL AND LABORATORY MANIFESTATIONS (part 1)

items.title: Differentiating Pulmonary Nodules in Coccidioidomycosis
items.content: Pulmonary nodules from coccidioidomycosis can mimic malignancies but have distinct radiographic features.
keyPoints: Nodules are usually ≤4 cm, located in the upper lobes, and often multiple with microsatellite lesions on CT imaging.
Calcification is uncommon; nodules may enhance on positron emission tomography.
Biopsy may be required to distinguish coccidioidal nodules from malignancies.
items.reference: Coccidioidomycosis · CLINICAL AND LABORATORY MANIFESTATIONS (part 1)

items.title: Identifying Risk Factors for Disseminated Coccidioidomycosis
items.content: Certain populations are at higher risk for disseminated coccidioidomycosis following primary infection.
keyPoints: Dissemination occurs in ~1% of infected individuals, often within 6 months post-infection.

Higher risk groups include male patients (especially African and Filipino ancestry), those with depressed cellular immunity (e.g., HIV-1 with CD4 <250/μL), chronic glucocorticoid therapy recipients, solid-organ transplant recipients, and those on TNF-α antagonists or biological response modifiers.
Women in late pregnancy or postpartum are also at increased risk.
items.reference: Coccidioidomycosis · CLINICAL AND LABORATORY MANIFESTATIONS (part 1)

items.title: Recognizing Coccidioidal Meningitis: Clinical and CSF Findings
items.content: Coccidioidal meningitis is a severe form of disseminated coccidioidomycosis, characterized by persistent headaches, lethargy, confusion, and mild nuchal rigidity. Diagnosis is supported by CSF findings such as lymphocytic pleocytosis, profound hypoglycorrhachia, elevated protein levels, and occasionally eosinophilia. Coccidioidal complement-fixing antibody in the CSF confirms meningitis.
keyPoints: Coccidioidal meningitis is uniformly fatal if untreated.
CSF findings: lymphocytic pleocytosis, profound hypoglycorrhachia, elevated protein levels.
Coccidioidal complement-fixing antibody in CSF confirms diagnosis.
items.reference: Coccidioidomycosis · CLINICAL AND LABORATORY MANIFESTATIONS (part 2) | DIAGNOSIS

items.title: Serological Diagnosis of Coccidioidomycosis: Techniques and Interpretation
items.content: Serology is the primary method for diagnosing coccidioidomycosis. Enzyme immunoassays (EIAs) for IgM and IgG are recommended due to their rapid turnaround and sensitivity. Positive EIA results should be confirmed with immunodiffusion or traditional assays. CF titers, particularly those ≥1:32, indicate active disease and potential extrathoracic dissemination.
keyPoints: EIAs for IgM and IgG are preferred for initial diagnosis.
Positive EIA results need confirmation by IDTP/IDCF or TP/CF assays.
CF titer ≥1:32 suggests active disease and possible dissemination.
items.reference: Coccidioidomycosis · CLINICAL AND LABORATORY MANIFESTATIONS (part 2) | DIAGNOSIS

items.title: Laboratory Safety and Diagnostic Techniques for Coccidioidomycosis
items.content: Coccidioides can be cultured from respiratory samples within 3–7 days at 37°C. Laboratory personnel must be alerted to prevent inhalation hazards. Direct identification of spherules in respiratory fluids or tissues using specific stains aids diagnosis. Antigenuria and antigenemia tests are useful for immunosuppressed patients with severe disease.
keyPoints: Coccidioides grows on blood agar within 3–7 days at 37°C.
Use Papanicolaou, Gomori methenamine silver, or calcofluor white staining to identify spherules.
Antigenuria and antigenemia tests are valuable for immunosuppressed patients.
items.reference: Coccidioidomycosis · CLINICAL AND LABORATORY MANIFESTATIONS (part 2) | DIAGNOSIS

items.title: Antifungal Therapy for Coccidioidomycosis: When and How to Treat
items.content: The treatment approach for coccidioidomycosis varies based on the severity of the disease. Amphotericin B is reserved for severe cases, including coccidioidal meningitis when other treatments fail. Triazole antifungals like fluconazole and itraconazole are preferred for most cases due to their efficacy and safety profile.
keyPoints: Amphotericin B (deoxycholate formulation) is administered intravenously at 0.7–1.0 mg/kg daily or three times per week, reserved for severe dissemination and coccidioidal meningitis when other therapies fail.
Lipid-based formulations of amphotericin B are less nephrotoxic but lack comparative efficacy data in coccidioidomycosis.

Triazole antifungals (fluconazole, itraconazole) are principal drugs for most cases; fluconazole is often preferred due to cost and absorption, while itraconazole may be more effective for bone/joint disease.
For triazoles: minimal adult oral dose is 400 mg/d; maximum itraconazole dose is 200 mg three times daily; higher fluconazole doses (up to 800 mg) are used for meningitis.
Voriconazole and posaconazole are alternatives for refractory cases, including meningitis.
High-dose triazoles may be teratogenic in the first trimester; avoid during this period.
items.reference: Coccidioidomycosis · Coccidioidomycosis

items.title: Indications and Criteria for Antifungal Therapy in Coccidioidomycosis
items.content: Antifungal therapy is not routinely required for focal primary pulmonary coccidioidomycosis but should be considered for patients with underlying immunodeficiencies or extensive disease. Specific criteria include persistent symptoms, night sweats, weight loss, high antibody titers, and radiographic evidence of extensive pulmonary involvement.
keyPoints: Most patients with focal primary pulmonary coccidioidomycosis do not need antifungal therapy.
Consider antifungal therapy for patients with underlying cellular immunodeficiencies or prolonged symptoms/signs of extensive disease.
Criteria for antifungal consideration: symptoms ≥2 months, night sweats >3 weeks, weight loss >10%, serum CF antibody titer >1:16, and extensive pulmonary involvement on chest radiography.
Appropriate therapy duration with fluconazole or itraconazole is 6 weeks to 6 months at 400 mg daily.
items.reference: Coccidioidomycosis · Coccidioidomycosis

items.explanation: Coccidioides organisms exist as filamentous molds or mycelia on agar media and in the environment.

items.question: Which structure is unique to Coccidioides once arthroconidia are inhaled by a susceptible host?
items.explanation: Spherules, which develop from arthroconidia after inhalation, are unique to Coccidioides.

items.question: What is the typical immune response in patients with resolved pulmonary coccidioidomycosis?
items.explanation: Patients with resolved pulmonary infection typically have necrotizing granulomas containing spherules.

items.question: Which symptom is most commonly associated with primary pulmonary coccidioidomycosis?
items.explanation: Fever, cough, and pleuritic chest pain are symptoms related primarily to pulmonary infection in primary coccidioidomycosis.

items.question: What is a common cutaneous manifestation of primary pulmonary coccidioidomycosis?
items.explanation: A diffuse, erythematous maculopapular rash known as toxic erythema is a common cutaneous manifestation.

items.question: Which imaging finding may suggest coccidioidomycosis rather than malignancy?

items.question: What is a risk factor for clinical dissemination of coccidioidomycosis?
items.explanation: Dissemination is more likely to occur in male patients, particularly those of African and possibly Filipino ancestry.
items.reference: Coccidioidomycosis · CLINICAL AND LABORATORY MANIFESTATIONS (part 1)

items.question: Which of the following is NOT a typical finding in the cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) of patients with coccidioidal meningitis?
items.explanation: Coccidioidal meningitis typically shows lymphocytic pleocytosis, not neutrophilic.

items.explanation: The presence of coccidioidal complement-fixing antibody in CSF is indicative of coccidioidal meningitis.

items.question: Which serological test is recommended for the initial diagnosis of coccidioidomycosis due to its higher sensitivity?

items.question: What is the significance of a CF titer $≥1:32$ in coccidioidomycosis?
options: It indicates immunity to the infection.
It suggests unrestrained fungal growth and disease activity.
It confirms the presence of bacterial co-infection.
It is not useful for prognostic purposes.
items.explanation: A CF titer $≥1:32$ suggests unrestrained fungal growth, predicts disease activity, and indicates possible extrathoracic dissemination.

items.question: Which antifungal agent is reserved for severe cases of coccidioidal dissemination and intrathecal or intraventricular administration in patients with coccidioidal meningitis when other therapies have failed?
items.explanation: Amphotericin B is reserved for severe cases of dissemination and specific administration routes in coccidioidal meningitis.

items.question: Which triazole antifungal has been the preferred choice historically due to its availability, cost, predictable oral absorption, and perceived lack of severe adverse events?
items.explanation: Fluconazole has been the preferred triazole historically for these reasons.

items.question: Which antifungal agent is noted to be potentially teratogenic during the first trimester of pregnancy and should be avoided?
items.explanation: High-dose triazole therapy, including fluconazole, may be teratogenic during the first trimester.

items.assertion: Coccidioidomycosis is confined to the Western Hemisphere.
items.reason: The fungus inhabits native soils within endemic regions, which are located in the Western Hemisphere.
